era_context: The Cold War reaches its most dangerous point with the Cuban Missile Crisis in October. The Space Race accelerates with the Mariner 2 Venus mission and John Glenn's orbital flight. Decolonization continues across Africa and Asia, with Algeria gaining independence after a brutal war. The Civil Rights Movement in the U.S. intensifies with the integration of the University of Mississippi. The Beatles release their first album.

events.title: Mariner 2 spacecraft reaches Venus; first successful planetary probe
events.description: NASA's Mariner 2 spacecraft reaches Venus on December 14, 1962, becoming the first spacecraft to successfully reach another planet. Mariner 2 transmits data about Venus's atmosphere, temperature, and magnetic field, revealing that Venus is an extremely hostile environment with surface temperatures exceeding 400 degrees Celsius. The mission is a major achievement in planetary exploration and demonstrates the feasibility of interplanetary spaceflight. Mariner 2's success paves the way for future planetary missions.

sources.name: NASA Jet Propulsion Laboratory mission reports and press releases
Mariner 2 telemetry and data
David Harland, The Soviet Conquest of Space (2003)

events.certainty_note: Mariner 2's Venus encounter date is documented in NASA records.

events.title: Cuban Missile Crisis; nuclear standoff between U.S. and Soviet Union
events.description: The Cuban Missile Crisis occurs from October 16–28, 1962, when the United States discovers Soviet nuclear missiles in Cuba, just 90 miles from the U.S. coast. President John F. Kennedy orders a naval blockade of Cuba and demands the removal of the missiles. The crisis brings the world to the brink of nuclear war. Soviet Premier Nikita Khrushchev agrees to remove the missiles in exchange for a U.S. pledge not to invade Cuba and the secret removal of U.S. missiles from Turkey. The crisis is resolved peacefully on October 28, 1962. The Cuban Missile Crisis is the closest the world comes to nuclear war during the Cold War and leads to a thaw in U.S.-Soviet relations.
key_figures: Robert F. Kennedy (U.S. Attorney General, President's brother)

sources.name: U.S. government records and declassified documents
Soviet government records (declassified)
Robert F. Kennedy, Thirteen Days (1969)
Odd Arne Westad, The Cold War: A World History (2017)

events.certainty_note: The Cuban Missile Crisis dates and key events are documented in U.S. government records and declassified Soviet documents.

events.title: John Glenn orbits Earth; first American to orbit the planet
events.description: NASA astronaut John Glenn launches aboard Mercury 6 on February 20, 1962, becoming the first American to orbit Earth. Glenn completes three orbits of Earth in 4 hours and 55 minutes. The mission is a major achievement for the U.S. space program and demonstrates American technological capabilities in the Space Race. Glenn's flight comes nearly a year after Soviet cosmonaut Yuri Gagarin's first human spaceflight (April 1961) and Soviet cosmonaut Gherman Titov's orbital flight (August 1961). Glenn's successful mission boosts American morale and confidence in the space program.

sources.name: NASA mission reports and press releases
John Glenn, Memoir (1999)
David Baker, The History of NASA's Space Shuttle Program (2011)

events.certainty_note: John Glenn's orbital flight date and mission duration are documented in NASA records.

events.title: Scott Carpenter orbits Earth; second American orbital flight
events.description: NASA astronaut Scott Carpenter launches aboard Mercury 7 on May 24, 1962, becoming the second American to orbit Earth. Carpenter completes three orbits of Earth in 4 hours and 56 minutes. The mission is successful, though Carpenter overshoots the landing zone, requiring a longer recovery operation. Carpenter's flight demonstrates the reliability of the Mercury spacecraft and continues the U.S. effort to catch up with the Soviet space program.

sources.name: NASA mission reports and press releases
Scott Carpenter, For Spacious Skies (2003)
David Baker, The History of NASA's Space Shuttle Program (2011)

events.certainty_note: Scott Carpenter's orbital flight date and mission duration are documented in NASA records.

events.title: Algeria gains independence from France after eight-year war
events.description: Algeria gains independence from France on July 5, 1962, following the Algerian War of Independence (1954–1962). The war is one of the most brutal decolonization conflicts, resulting in an estimated 1 million deaths. The independence is achieved through the Evian Accords, signed on March 18, 1962, which provide for a ceasefire and the withdrawal of French forces. Ahmed Ben Bella becomes the first President of independent Algeria. Algeria's independence marks the end of French colonial rule in North Africa and is a major victory for the anti-colonial movement.

sources.name: Algerian independence proclamation and constitutional documents
Evian Accords (treaty document)
Alistair Horne, A Savage War of Peace: Algeria 1954–1962 (1977)

events.certainty_note: Algeria's independence date and first President are documented in official government records.

events.title: James Meredith integrates University of Mississippi; federal intervention required
events.description: James Meredith, an African American student, enrolls at the University of Mississippi on October 1, 1962, becoming the first African American to attend the university. The enrollment triggers violent protests by white students and residents. President John F. Kennedy orders federal marshals and National Guard troops to enforce Meredith's right to attend. The integration of the University of Mississippi is a major victory for the Civil Rights Movement and demonstrates the federal government's commitment to enforcing civil rights. However, the violence and resistance highlight the depth of racial prejudice in the South.

sources.name: U.S. Department of Justice records and federal marshal reports
Contemporary newspaper coverage and photographs
James Meredith, A Mission from God (2012)
Taylor Branch, Parting the Waters: America in the King Years 1954–63 (1988)

events.certainty_note: Meredith's enrollment date and the federal intervention are documented in government records and contemporary sources.

events.title: The Beatles release their first album; 'Please Please Me'
events.description: The Beatles release their first album, 'Please Please Me,' on March 22, 1962 (in the UK). The album includes the hit single 'Please Please Me' and other songs that establish the Beatles as a major force in popular music. The album is a commercial and critical success, reaching number one on the UK charts. The Beatles' early success in the UK sets the stage for their later dominance of American popular music and the British Invasion of 1964.
key_figures: Ringo Starr (The Beatles, joined in August 1962)

sources.name: The Beatles, 'Please Please Me' album (recorded and released)
Contemporary British press coverage
Mark Lewisohn, The Beatles: All These Years (2013)

events.certainty_note: The Beatles' first album release date is documented in music industry records.
cross_references: 1964-005 (The Beatles perform on Ed Sullivan Show)

events.title: Ringo Starr joins The Beatles; replaces Pete Best
events.description: Ringo Starr (Richard Starkey) joins The Beatles on August 16, 1962, replacing Pete Best as the drummer. Ringo's addition completes the classic Beatles lineup of John Lennon, Paul McCartney, George Harrison, and Ringo Starr. Ringo's drumming style and personality contribute to the Beatles' success and help establish their distinctive sound. Ringo's joining marks the beginning of the Beatles' rise to international fame.

sources.name: The Beatles' management records and press releases
Contemporary British press coverage
Mark Lewisohn, The Beatles: All These Years (2013)

events.certainty_note: Ringo Starr's joining date is documented in The Beatles' management records.
cross_references: 1964-005 (The Beatles perform on Ed Sullivan Show)

events.title: Telstar satellite launched; first transatlantic television transmission
events.description: The Telstar satellite is launched on July 10, 1962, becoming the first active communications satellite. Telstar enables the first live transatlantic television transmission on July 23, 1962, when a television signal is transmitted from the United States to France and then to the United Kingdom. The Telstar satellite is a major achievement in telecommunications and demonstrates the feasibility of satellite communications. Telstar's success leads to the development of a global satellite communications network.
key_figures: Bell Telephone Laboratories scientists and engineers

sources.name: Bell Telephone Laboratories press releases and technical reports
Contemporary newspaper coverage
David Harland, The Soviet Conquest of Space (2003)

events.certainty_note: Telstar's launch date and first transatlantic transmission date are documented in Bell Telephone Laboratories records.

events.title: Sino-Indian War; border conflict between China and India
events.description: The Sino-Indian War occurs from October 20 to November 21, 1962, as a result of border disputes between China and India in the Himalayan region. China launches a military offensive against Indian forces, achieving significant victories. India suffers a humiliating defeat, with over 3,000 soldiers killed or captured. The war is a major blow to Indian prestige and to Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru's policy of non-alignment. The war also marks a turning point in Sino-Indian relations, establishing China as a major regional power and India as a secondary power in Asia.

sources.name: Indian and Chinese government statements and military reports
Contemporary newspaper coverage
Neville Maxwell, India's China War (1970)

events.certainty_note: The Sino-Indian War dates and casualty figures are documented in government records and contemporary sources.

events.title: Vatican II opens; Catholic Church begins modernization
events.description: The Second Vatican Council (Vatican II) opens on October 11, 1962, under Pope John XXIII. Vatican II is a major ecumenical council of the Catholic Church that aims to modernize the Church and improve relations with other Christian denominations and religions. The council introduces significant changes to Catholic practice, including the use of vernacular languages in the Mass (instead of Latin), greater emphasis on ecumenism, and reforms to Church governance. Vatican II has profound implications for the Catholic Church and Christianity as a whole, leading to significant changes in Catholic theology and practice.
key_figures: Pope John XXIII (Pope, convened Vatican II)
Cardinal Giuseppe Roncalli (Pope John XXIII's birth name)

sources.name: Vatican II documents and council records
Contemporary Catholic press coverage
John W. O'Malley, What Happened at Vatican II (2008)

events.certainty_note: Vatican II's opening date is documented in Vatican records.

events.title: Nelson Mandela arrested in South Africa; begins 27-year imprisonment
events.description: Nelson Mandela is arrested on August 5, 1962, in South Africa, charged with sabotage and conspiracy. Mandela is sentenced to five years in prison, which he serves on Robben Island. In 1964, Mandela is tried again and sentenced to life imprisonment on charges of sabotage and conspiracy. Mandela spends 27 years in prison, becoming a symbol of resistance to apartheid. His imprisonment galvanizes international opposition to apartheid and makes him a global icon of the anti-apartheid movement.

sources.name: South African government records and court documents
Nelson Mandela, Long Walk to Freedom (1994)
Anthony Sampson, Mandela: The Authorized Biography (1999)

events.certainty_note: Mandela's arrest date and charges are documented in South African government records.

disconfirming_evidence: The Cuban Missile Crisis is sometimes portrayed as a clear-cut U.S. victory; in fact, the U.S. agreed to remove missiles from Turkey as part of the settlement, though this was kept secret at the time. The Sino-Indian War is sometimes characterized as a decisive Chinese victory; in fact, China withdrew after achieving its objectives, suggesting the war was more limited in scope than often portrayed. James Meredith's integration of the University of Mississippi is sometimes portrayed as a straightforward victory for civil rights; in fact, Meredith faced ongoing harassment and discrimination after his enrollment. The Beatles' early success is sometimes attributed solely to their musical talent; in fact, their success was also due to effective marketing, radio promotion, and the cultural moment of the early 1960s.
historiographic_note: 1962 is exceptionally well-documented due to proximity to the present and the richness of contemporary media, government records, and later oral histories. Western sources dominate the record, particularly for U.S. and European events. The Cuban Missile Crisis is extensively documented in U.S. government records and declassified Soviet documents. The Space Race is well-documented due to public interest and declassification of government records. The Algerian War is extensively documented in French and Algerian sources, though Western historiography often emphasizes the French perspective. The Sino-Indian War is less densely documented in English-language historiography, though primary sources exist in Indian and Chinese archives. The Beatles' early career is well-documented in music industry records and contemporary press coverage.

graph_edges.note: Mariner 2 Venus mission and Cuban Missile Crisis occur in same year
Cuban Missile Crisis leads to Limited Test Ban Treaty
John Glenn and Scott Carpenter orbital flights occur in same year
Algeria's independence leads to Ahmed Ben Bella's presidency
University of Mississippi integration leads to March on Washington
Beatles' first album and Ringo Starr joining occur in same year
Ringo Starr joining leads to Beatles' Ed Sullivan Show performance
Sino-Indian War leads to Indo-Pakistani War tensions
Mandela's arrest leads to 27-year imprisonment and later release
